Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":30180279,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMDE4MDI3OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/54%20-%20Needs%20decision","name":"54 - Needs decision","color":"207de5","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-02-13T01:01:26Z","updated_at":"2024-09-09T10:59:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There is a [specific implementation for binary search](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.searchsorted.html).  Can there also be one for linear search?  There are use cases where the first element where [condition] might frequently be very close to the front of the array, but existing methods for executing a linear search in numpy require operations on the entire array.  There seems to be [some demand for this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53020764/efficiently-return-the-index-of-the-first-value-satisfying-condition-in-array) (see also linked questions).  I can be convinced to work on this, but I've never contributed, and would appreciate some tips on where to start with this.

Related:  https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/1835","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23203/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23203/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2337801704","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/23203#issuecomment-2337801704","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23203","id":2337801704,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86LWAXo","user":{"login":"krisutofu","id":64962929,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY0OTYyOTI5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/64962929?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu","html_url":"https://github.com/krisutofu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/krisutofu/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-09T10:59:31Z","updated_at":"2024-09-09T10:59:31Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Over one year later and I wonder, wouldn't it be the easiest way to allow `argwhere()` (and potentially other numpy functions) to specify an output length which is used to break a loop internally?

At the moment, linear search is easiest with

```py
index = numpy.argwhere(array == 3)[0]   # row from result
```

I don't know, what happens internally but it could compare all elements and index all non-zeros before it returns the first index. This is great for impeding side-channel attacks but bad for performance.

For example a faster linear search:
```py
index = numpy.argwhere(array == 3, at=0)
```

It might still compute all comparisons but at least stops when it finds the first non-zero value. It could also be a `length=` parameter which would stop after having found the given number of non-zeros.

Just mentioning this for others who stumble upon this issue. I don't know if the actual benefit of such an extra parameter is worth its implementation effort.
